claim: Build smaller decomposed sub-tasks for agents when they fail a larger task, rather than just re-prompting; this unblocks capability gains by letting agents learn to reassemble complex work from building blocks.
actionable: When an agentic coding task fails, analyze what capability/context/structure is missing, then create scaffolding (smaller sub-tasks, helper functions, clearer interfaces) so the agent can solve those first and reassemble into the larger objective.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Constrain build times to force architectural discipline and prevent slow creep; when agents encounter time limits (e.g., 1-minute max), they naturally push back on scope and decompose the build graph, keeping code more maintainable.
actionable: Set a hard build-time envelope (e.g., 1 minute) and enforce it with agents; when builds exceed it, decompose the task graph rather than allowing creep, so you maintain consistent build guarantees.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: The only scarce resource in AI-driven development is human attention, not tokens/GPU capacity; design the entire system to minimize synchronous human involvement and maximize agent parallelization.
actionable: Identify synchronous human bottlenecks in your development loop (PRs, reviews, context-switching) and build automation to remove them; invest agents to handle the work instead of optimizing for human comfort.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Everything the agent needs is text: prompts, docs, tests, lint rules, review comments. Systematically harvest feedback (failed PRs, bugs, code review notes) and encode it back into markdown/docs as narrative context for the agent.
actionable: When an agent makes a mistake or receives feedback, distill the lesson into a markdown doc (e.g., observability requirements, code patterns, guardrails) and inject it into the agent context; treat feedback as a source of permanent institutional knowledge.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Don't scaffold agents with predefined state machines; reasoning models perform better with full context and optionality, letting them choose how to proceed rather than forcing them through rigid workflows.
actionable: For reasoning models (vs. supervised agents), provide broad context (skills, scripts, observability) and let the model decide when to use which tool; minimize rigid scaffolds that restrict choice.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Post-merge code review is viable at scale when agents are doing most authorship; spot-check samples to infer team struggles, then feed insights back into lint/prompts rather than blocking every PR.
actionable: Move code review post-merge for AI-authored PRs; sample and analyze for patterns of misalignment, then encode corrective prompts/lints back into the harness so the agent learns without human synchronous review.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Agents need observability hooks (logging, tracing, metrics dashboards) to self-diagnose and improve; without visibility into what went wrong, agents can't learn to produce more reliable code.
actionable: Invest in lightweight observability (Prometheus, Jaeger, structured logging) and give agents read access to traces; let them review failures and propose observability improvements (new metrics, dashboards) alongside code.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: CLI tools that are structured, minimal-output, and agent-friendly (e.g., with --silent flags) are 10x more efficient for agents than human-friendly GUIs or verbose output; redesign CLIs for agent consumption.
actionable: When building CLIs, add flags like --silent, --json, --exit-code-only that suppress human-oriented output and surface only the signal (pass/fail, errors); agents will parse this in fewer tokens.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: For reasoning models, convert visual/UI information to ASCII art or text descriptions; agents don't perceive visually the same way humans do (they see latent embeddings, not colors/shapes).
actionable: When agents need to understand UI/layout, rasterize images to ASCII art or structured text rather than passing raw images; this aligns better with how language models perceive spatial information.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Internalizing dependencies (vendoring small/medium-sized ones, ~2k LOC) is cheaper and lower-friction than managing external updates when tokens are cheap; you strip unneeded generality and customize for your use case.
actionable: For dependencies under ~2k LOC, evaluate internalizing them: write them directly into your codebase tailored to your needs, securing them deeply with internal security audits rather than waiting for upstream patches.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Code is disposable when it's trivially regenerable; this mindset frees you to fail faster, iterate parallel branches, and prioritize system design over preserving human-authored code.
actionable: When authoring code with agents, embrace disposability: spawn multiple parallel attempts, prefer starting over to slow convergence, invest in system design that lets agents regenerate code cheaply.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Models are good at following instructions; encode all non-functional requirements (reliability, observability, architecture) as prompts, lints, tests, and guardrails rather than rigid scaffolds.
actionable: Instead of building restrictive scaffolds around agentic code generation, encode requirements as clear instructions in prompts and lint rules; let the model demonstrate compliance through its output.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Give agents full access to their domain; avoid boxing them with overly restrictive permissions. They perform better when they can see traces, logs, and have tools available to reason about and solve problems holistically.
actionable: Design agent permissions broadly within their domain (code, logs, traces, dashboards, deployment tools); resist the urge to restrict; trust them to use context wisely when they have enough visibility.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: GPT-5.4 supports 1M token context and reasoning at scale; the longer you can run an agent before context compaction, the better it performs on complex tasks.
actionable: When choosing a model for agentic tasks, prioritize large context windows (1M+ tokens); longer continuous runs reduce recompaction and improve agent coherence on multi-step problems.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: GPT-5.3 introduced background shells, allowing agents to spawn long-running processes asynchronously; this requires rearchitecting tooling (make → Bazel → Turbo → NX) to complete within tight latency budgets.
actionable: When agents gain background process capability, audit build systems for latency; you may need to migrate to faster tooling (Turbo/NX) to keep feedback loops under 1 minute so agents stay responsive.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Spark (a smaller, faster model) is good for quick spike work and documentation updates but struggles with complex multi-step tasks; use different models for different task types rather than one-size-fits-all.
actionable: For quick edits and documentation, use a smaller fast model like Spark; for complex code generation and reasoning tasks, use larger models (GPT-5.4); don't force all tasks through the same model.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: New product/greenfield work remains a hard task for agents; they excel at code generation in existing codebases but struggle with zero-to-one ideation and high-level design without heavy human steering.
actionable: For new products, provide agents with templates, mockups, and design specs to ground ideation; don't expect agents to drive zero-to-one discovery alone—pair them with human product vision.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Large-scale refactoring is harder for agents than incremental coding; they struggle more with scope-blowing changes where interfaces are undefined. Focus human effort there; let agents handle smaller, clearer tasks.
actionable: Reserve complex refactorings and architectural redesigns for human-led work; use agents for incremental, well-scoped changes; when refactoring is necessary, break it into smaller subtasks for agents.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is token-inefficient for agentic use; it forces all tool definitions into context, making tokens expensive. Build lightweight CLI shims instead to expose only what agents actually need.
actionable: Avoid MCP for agent tools; instead, wrap complex tools in simple CLI interfaces that expose only essential commands and output minimal context.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Give agents skills as optional tools they can decide when to use (not mandatory scaffolds); combine with prompts that let them push back on guidance (e.g., 'bias toward merging unless P0-level issues').
actionable: Design agent prompts with built-in optionality: 'defer non-critical feedback to backlog, merge if P1-or-lower', allowing agents to balance perfection against forward momentum.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: 'Ghost libraries' (specs + reference implementations) are a scalable way to distribute reusable agent-understandable scaffolding; agents can implement the spec locally, avoiding monolithic dependencies.
actionable: Instead of publishing traditional libraries, publish specs: high-level requirements and reference implementations in existing codebases; agents can regenerate specialized versions for their own codebase.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Elixir/BEAM's process supervision is well-suited for orchestrating large numbers of agent tasks (tens/hundreds in parallel); the actor model and gen_server pattern align naturally with agentic job choreography.
actionable: If building agentic coordination systems (orchestration of many agents), evaluate Elixir for its native concurrency/supervision primitives; it reduces boilerplate vs. other languages.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review

claim: Merge conflicts are not a blocker at scale when agents resolve them; agents excel at conflict resolution in multi-agent repos. Don't avoid git worktrees—use them with agent-driven merge conflict handling.
actionable: Use git worktrees for parallel agent work; when PRs conflict, delegate conflict resolution to an agent-driven skill rather than manually rebasing.
source_title: Extreme Harness Engineering: 1M LOC, 1B toks/day, 0% human code or review
